result:
:::writing
At the edge of a small town, there’s a vending machine that hums even when it’s unplugged. People say it used to sit in a train station that burned down years ago, but somehow it showed up again behind a laundromat, as if it had simply walked there overnight.

No one remembers stocking it. Still, its glass front is always full. Not with chips or soda, but with things that feel oddly specific: a key with no label, a postcard from a city you’ve never visited, a watch ticking a few seconds too slow. The prices change depending on who’s looking. Sometimes it asks for coins, sometimes for something less clear.

A teenager once fed it a crumpled receipt and got back a photograph of himself as a child, standing beside a dog he swore he’d never owned. An old woman pressed her palm against the buttons and received a neatly folded apology note in handwriting she hadn’t seen in decades.

Most people only use it once.

There’s a quiet understanding in town that the machine doesn’t give you what you want. It gives you what lingers. The things that hover just out of reach, that tug at you in idle moments.

At night, when the streetlights flicker, the machine glows faintly, like it’s thinking. Like it’s waiting for the next person to realize that some exchanges don’t need money—just a willingness to see what you’ve been carrying all along.
:::

content.text:
:::writing
At the edge of a small town, there’s a vending machine that hums even when it’s unplugged. People say it used to sit in a train station that burned down years ago, but somehow it showed up again behind a laundromat, as if it had simply walked there overnight.

No one remembers stocking it. Still, its glass front is always full. Not with chips or soda, but with things that feel oddly specific: a key with no label, a postcard from a city you’ve never visited, a watch ticking a few seconds too slow. The prices change depending on who’s looking. Sometimes it asks for coins, sometimes for something less clear.

A teenager once fed it a crumpled receipt and got back a photograph of himself as a child, standing beside a dog he swore he’d never owned. An old woman pressed her palm against the buttons and received a neatly folded apology note in handwriting she hadn’t seen in decades.

Most people only use it once.

There’s a quiet understanding in town that the machine doesn’t give you what you want. It gives you what lingers. The things that hover just out of reach, that tug at you in idle moments.

At night, when the streetlights flicker, the machine glows faintly, like it’s thinking. Like it’s waiting for the next person to realize that some exchanges don’t need money—just a willingness to see what you’ve been carrying all along.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
